# Preface {.unnumbered}

This is the course reader for *Bio196-22 Computing for Bioinformatics*. It's organized around what you'll actually need week by week as the course unfolds — a focused six-week companion to the broader computing world.

You'll learn the practical side of bioinformatics: the Unix command line, editing project files, connecting to remote systems, submitting jobs to compute clusters, managing software environments, working with containers, understanding sequencing data, building reproducible pipelines, and sharing your analyses clearly.

## Who This Is For

If you're a graduate student or researcher starting in computational biology, a wet-lab scientist wanting to analyze your own sequencing data, a programmer moving into life sciences, or an HPC administrator supporting bioinformatics users, this course is designed for you.

## What You'll Learn

By the end of this course, you'll be comfortable with the Unix command line — navigating, manipulating files, and editing plain-text project files. You'll connect to remote systems with SSH and submit jobs to Slurm clusters. You'll manage software environments with Conda and package analyses with Docker and Apptainer. You'll understand sequencing data and RNA-seq workflows, run reproducible Nextflow pipelines, and track your work with Git and GitHub. And you'll know how to produce clear, reproducible reports with Quarto.

## How It's Organized

Each week builds on the last.

**Week 1** covers Linux basics — the shell, files, text processing, shell scripting, and editing.

**Week 2** introduces SSH and cluster computing, where you'll submit real jobs to a scheduler.

**Week 3** tackles software management: Conda environments and containers (Docker, Apptainer).

**Week 4** connects computing to actual sequencing data and walks through a standard RNA-seq analysis.

**Week 5** scales up to Nextflow and nf-core pipelines, letting you build and run reproducible workflows.

**Week 6** wraps up with Git for version control and Quarto for reports that document your work.

## How to Use This

Work through the chapters in order during the course. Each one has explanations, examples, and exercises. After the course, you'll return to specific chapters as references when setting up new projects or debugging problems.

We don't assume any prior programming experience. Some basic biology knowledge (genes, sequences, expression data) will help you follow the examples, but it's not essential.

---

*Happy computing!*

— Yucheng Zhang and Shirley Li, Tufts University
